front: What does Cantor's Theorem state about the power set of a set?
back: Cantor's Theorem states that for any set S, the cardinality of its power set P(S) is strictly greater than the cardinality of S itself (|P(S)| > |S|).

front: What is the core argument used in the proof of Cantor's Theorem?
back: The proof uses a diagonalization argument. It assumes a function f: S -> P(S) exists and constructs a new subset D of S defined as D = { x ∈ S | x ∉ f(x) }. This set D cannot be in the image of f, leading to a contradiction and proving no surjection exists.

front: What important consequence does Cantor's Theorem have for infinite sets?
back: It shows there is no 'largest' infinity or cardinal number. Given any infinite set, its power set has a strictly larger cardinality, leading to an infinite hierarchy of infinities (e.g., |ℕ| < |P(ℕ)| < |P(P(ℕ))| < ...).

front: How does Cantor's Theorem relate to the concept of a 'universal set'?
back: Cantor's Theorem implies that a universal set containing all other sets cannot exist. If such a set U existed, then P(U) would be a subset of U (by definition of universal set), meaning |P(U)| ≤ |U|, which directly contradicts the theorem.

front: In the context of metric spaces, what is a key implication of Cantor's Theorem regarding the real numbers?
back: Cantor's Theorem proves that the cardinality of the real numbers (the continuum) is strictly greater than that of the natural numbers. This is because the power set of the natural numbers, P(ℕ), can be put into a bijection with the real numbers, so |ℝ| = |P(ℕ)| > |ℕ|.
